title: A Day Aboard the Station
tagline: Three hundred miles up, a full EVA on the schedule, and no room for error.

wake_up.text: The cabin lights fade up slowly, no alarm needed at this altitude — your body just knows. Outside the window, sunrise number one of sixteen today. Mission control's first message is already waiting: today's EVA is confirmed.

choices.text: Take a few minutes to stretch and orient before anything else
Get straight into the task list

system_check.text: Overnight telemetry is in: life support nominal, one solar array reading slightly off-angle. Nothing urgent, but worth a look.

choices.text: Run a full diagnostic on the array before breakfast
Note it in the log and move on

morning_briefing.text: Mission control's voice crackles in: today's EVA is a repair on a coolant line, plus a materials experiment with a narrow window. "Suit up by 09:00," they say. "We'd like this done before the next comms blackout."

choices.text: Copy that

exercise_block.text: Two hours on the resistance machine, mandatory in microgravity or your bones pay for it later. Today it feels like it's taking forever.

choices.text: Push through the entire two hours properly
Cut it short to bank time for prep

eva_prep.text: Suit-up begins. Your crewmate hands you the checklist — sixty items, and every one of them exists because someone, once, skipped it.

choices.text: Go through every item yourself, even the ones your crewmate already checked
Trust yesterday's inspection and move quickly

eva_start.text: The airlock cycles and Earth swings into view, enormous and silent. Forty minutes into the coolant repair, tethered and focused, your suit's CO2 scrubber alarm chirps once, then again.

choices.text: Check the readout

eva_alarm.text: "CO2 levels climbing," your suit computer reports flatly. "Estimated eighteen minutes to threshold." You're two bolts from finishing the repair. Every second you spend deciding is a second the scrubber doesn't have.

choices.text: Finish the repair first — you're almost done
Switch to the backup scrubber cartridge and keep working
Radio mission control and let them walk you through it
Abort the repair and head straight back to the airlock

eva_outcome_finish.text: You finish with your ears ringing and the alarm blaring the whole time. The repair holds. Mission control's silence on the channel says more than a reprimand would.

choices.text: Head back inside

eva_outcome_backup.text: The backup cartridge seats with a click and the numbers start falling almost immediately. You finish the repair with room to breathe — literally.

choices.text: Head back inside

eva_outcome_radio.text: Ground talks you through a scrubber reset in real time, calm and precise. It works. It's exactly what the channel is for, and everyone on it knows it.

choices.text: Head back inside

eva_outcome_abort.text: You cycle back through the airlock with the repair unfinished. Safe, breathing easy, and aware that tomorrow's EVA schedule just got a new line item.

choices.text: Get out of the suit

post_eva.text: Suit off, water first, then the debrief. Ground wants a full report on the scrubber event.

choices.text: Write the full, detailed incident report
Log a short summary and move on

experiment_time.text: The materials experiment has a narrow window before the station's orbit puts it in shadow. You're behind schedule after the EVA.

choices.text: Push to get it done in the window
Let it slip to tomorrow and rest

comms_window.text: Your scheduled call home comes up on the manifest. Ten minutes, maybe fifteen if you're efficient with everything else.

choices.text: Take the full call, whatever else waits
Keep it short to get back to the task list

equipment_glitch.text: A cabin-wide CO2 sensor throws a fault code — probably a sensor glitch, but "probably" isn't a word anyone likes hearing about air quality.

choices.text: Manually verify air quality yourself before trusting the sensor
Wait for ground control to confirm it's a false alarm

dinner_prep.text: Rehydrated stew, again, but the crew's gathered around the galley table — as much as anyone gathers around anything in zero-g.

choices.text: Eat with the crew and decompress
Eat quickly at your station and keep working

sleep_prep.text: Log time. Ground wants a handoff summary before the next shift's morning briefing.

choices.text: Write a complete, detailed handoff
Keep it brief — tomorrow's crew can ask if they need to

end_shift.text: Lights dim for the sleep period. Sixteen sunrises today, and you saw maybe three of them properly.

choices.text: Strap in and sleep

random_moment.text: Between the briefing and the day's first real task, a few minutes pass the way they always do up here — never quite the same twice.

choices.text: A crewmate shares a rare fresh snack from the last resupply
A minor system alarm blips and clears itself
The sunrise from the cupola is unusually striking today

triumphant.title: Textbook Orbit
triumphant.blurb: Every system nominal, every objective met. Ground control's sign-off message tonight has an exclamation point in it, which from them, means everything.

steady_hand.title: Solid Rotation
steady_hand.blurb: Nothing dramatic, nothing missed. The kind of day that makes you exactly the crewmate everyone wants on the next mission.

ordinary_day.title: Just Another Orbit
ordinary_day.blurb: Sixteen sunrises, one repair, one experiment, one very long checklist. Tomorrow brings sixteen more.

burned_out.title: Redlining
burned_out.blurb: You made it to sleep period, but your vitals log is going to raise an eyebrow at mission control tomorrow. Pace yourself.

written_up.title: Debrief Pending
written_up.blurb: A few calls today are going into tomorrow's mission review. Nobody was hurt — but ground wants a conversation.
